_note: JSON sidecar for migration-manifest.yaml. The wizard runtime CLI consumes JSON; YAML is the human-facing companion. Both files MUST stay in sync.

artifact_notes.agents/lib/external_write/scan.py: Now also enforces a structural rule that capability and operator code can only reach the bulk-write engine through its small, approved front door (capability_api / operations / read_facade) — any other internal import from the engine's own package is refused, instead of relying only on catching specific known misuses one at a time.
artifact_notes.agents/lib/external_write/zones.py: Extends the internal trust-boundary registry so eight pre-existing internal support files are recognized as legitimate engine wiring under the new rule above, rather than being mistaken for a violation of it.

migrations.migration_steps: 1. Run the update check (it reports what's new + the recommendation). 2. On the operator's yes, the assistant applies it ("${{WIZARD_HOME:-$HOME/agent-wizard}}/scripts/wizard" self-upgrade --to v0.16.0 --apply). 3. A backup is taken first; the operator's own data, rules, credentials, and logs are never touched.
migrations.notes: Minor-additive, emitted-lib-only release to v0.16.0 (Cut 1.2 — core-integrity findings A' / V15-3b): an emitted-lib-only, minor-additive slice (Cut 1.2 — core-integrity findings A' / V15-3b) touching exactly two files in the emitted external_write lib (agents/lib/external_write/): scan.py adds a STRUCTURAL CAPABILITY-zone import-boundary check — capability/operator code may only reach the bulk-write engine through its small, approved allowlist (capability_api, operations, read_facade); any other internal external_write submodule import is now refused as a sealed_kernel_import bypass, closing the routing-invariant class (V15-3) at the module level instead of one remembered symbol at a time. zones.py extends the SEALED_KERNEL exemption registry with eight pre-existing deterministic, non-vendor, non-credential kernel support modules that the new rule would otherwise have flagged, so legitimate internal kernel wiring is recognized rather than mistaken for a violation. No other emitted content changes. No breaking changes; all existing capabilities are byte-for-byte unaffected and all prior operator project files remain valid.
migrations.preflight: Verify the operator project carries a .wizard/manifest.json whose foundation_bundle_version is v0.15.0 (or any version in the supported range). A backup is taken automatically.
migrations.rollback: Restore from .wizard/backups/pre-v0.16.0/ and set foundation_bundle_version back to v0.15.0.

migrations.migration_steps: 1. Run the update check (it reports what's new + the recommendation). 2. On the operator's yes, the assistant applies it ("${{WIZARD_HOME:-$HOME/agent-wizard}}/scripts/wizard" self-upgrade --to v0.16.0 --apply). 3. A backup is taken first; the operator's own data, rules, credentials, and logs are never touched.
migrations.notes: Minor-additive combined jump from v0.14.0 straight to v0.15.0: delivers everything through v0.12.0 (the RunEnvelope bulk-write trust core) PLUS the v0.13.0 operability slice (a turnkey operator-acceptance CLI (registered_adapters.py fires every adapter module's registration up front), the judgment-path triage tool (triage.py) bound to a versioned reviewed_set-v2 so an operator's approval is bound to exactly what was shown, a safe standing-automation entrypoint that never lets a scheduled job silently fall through to a live run on an unrecognized flag, and an offline OAuth scope grant-check (deny-by-default: verified only after a real, recorded use of exactly the declared scope) surfaced through the credential registry, Credential Setup, and the next-phase live-trial-readiness gate) PLUS the v0.13.1 patch (the upgrade reconcile now also scopes agents/capabilities/ so a retired-surface capability whose imports are broken is paused as import-broken rather than left merely "paused"; a new composite, read-only capability health-check primitive (capability_health.py) lets a session-start orientation check catch an import-broken capability before inviting the operator into it; the write_gate runtime now fails closed on a paused capability's op_kind on every sanctioned route; and the update-notice prose now separates the informational "see what's new" read from the separate, explicit approval an apply requires) PLUS the v0.14.0 Phase 3 Cut 1 self-QA slice (a fail-closed capability-identity resolver (capability_identity.py) enforcing a four-way descriptor/capability/mechanism/module-stem coherence check — surface deliberately excluded — at registration, acceptance, and health time, so a descriptor id that resolves to a different capability than the one being registered is refused rather than silently trusted; an idempotent lifecycle reconciler (lifecycle_state.py) that keeps paused/accepted state coherent (a rebuild or migration forces accepted:false until the operator re-trials it, and a conformant rebuild that changed the capability's own code is detected as stale and its acceptance revoked rather than carried forward silently) and appends to the acceptance audit log on every re-acceptance; and the D-Layer-1 deterministic capability-invariant battery plus test-quality probes (capability_invariants.py), run at next-phase Step 4 and fail-closed before any live trial. Also scrubs internal decision-record IDs out of emitted content and adds .gitignore coverage for the consent/runtime artifacts these mechanisms generate PLUS the Cut 1.1 slice: five sets of improvements to bulk-write reliability, capability-lifecycle testing, and audit trust. First, resuming an interrupted bulk run (a large batch of writes split into many smaller pieces) now picks up safely from exactly where it left off, using the one approval you already gave, recorded at the moment you gave it — it can never restart from the beginning, silently skip pieces, or ask you to approve the same batch twice. Second, a capability's own tests can no longer accidentally depend on leftover state from an unrelated earlier run, and building a new capability now proves — as a standard build-time check — that its tests would actually catch a real mistake, not just that they run without error; approving a capability also now cleans up any leftover internal marker it no longer needs, and a naming collision between a capability you have only described and one you have actually built is reported honestly rather than showing as broken. Third, finishing an approval that had a related pending update task now also completes that task instead of leaving it stranded, and a capability an update safely paused can be brought back through the same guided review-and-approve flow used to build a new one. Fourth, you can now ask your system, at any time, exactly what a bulk run wrote, what is recoverable, and whether it can confirm each write against the real account — without waiting on a scheduled check — and a handful of routine, read-only checks (that never write anything) no longer interrupt you for permission every single time; anything that writes anywhere still always asks first. Fifth, a privacy-safe, committable summary of each bulk run is now saved automatically — with no raw personal information in it — so you always have durable proof of what happened and that you approved it, and a run's outcome is now always described strictly from what actually happened, so it can never be rounded up to “all done” when it was not. PLUS the v0.16.0 Cut 1.2 slice: an emitted-lib-only, minor-additive slice (Cut 1.2 — core-integrity findings A' / V15-3b) touching exactly two files in the emitted external_write lib (agents/lib/external_write/): scan.py adds a STRUCTURAL CAPABILITY-zone import-boundary check — capability/operator code may only reach the bulk-write engine through its small, approved allowlist (capability_api, operations, read_facade); any other internal external_write submodule import is now refused as a sealed_kernel_import bypass, closing the routing-invariant class (V15-3) at the module level instead of one remembered symbol at a time. zones.py extends the SEALED_KERNEL exemption registry with eight pre-existing deterministic, non-vendor, non-credential kernel support modules that the new rule would otherwise have flagged, so legitimate internal kernel wiring is recognized rather than mistaken for a violation. No other emitted content changes.) No breaking changes; all existing capabilities are byte-for-byte unaffected and all prior operator project files remain valid.
migrations.preflight: Verify the operator project carries a .wizard/manifest.json whose foundation_bundle_version is v0.14.0 (or any version in the supported range). A backup is taken automatically.
migrations.rollback: Restore from .wizard/backups/pre-v0.16.0/ and set foundation_bundle_version back to v0.14.0.

migrations.notes: Minor-additive combined jump from v0.10.2 straight to v0.15.0: delivers everything through v0.12.0 (the RunEnvelope bulk-write trust core) PLUS the v0.13.0 operability slice (a turnkey operator-acceptance CLI (registered_adapters.py fires every adapter module's registration up front), the judgment-path triage tool (triage.py) bound to a versioned reviewed_set-v2 so an operator's approval is bound to exactly what was shown, a safe standing-automation entrypoint that never lets a scheduled job silently fall through to a live run on an unrecognized flag, and an offline OAuth scope grant-check (deny-by-default: verified only after a real, recorded use of exactly the declared scope) surfaced through the credential registry, Credential Setup, and the next-phase live-trial-readiness gate) PLUS the v0.13.1 patch (the upgrade reconcile now also scopes agents/capabilities/ so a retired-surface capability whose imports are broken is paused as import-broken rather than left merely "paused"; a new composite, read-only capability health-check primitive (capability_health.py) lets a session-start orientation check catch an import-broken capability before inviting the operator into it; the write_gate runtime now fails closed on a paused capability's op_kind on every sanctioned route; and the update-notice prose now separates the informational "see what's new" read from the separate, explicit approval an apply requires) PLUS the v0.14.0 Phase 3 Cut 1 self-QA slice (a fail-closed capability-identity resolver (capability_identity.py) enforcing a four-way descriptor/capability/mechanism/module-stem coherence check — surface deliberately excluded — at registration, acceptance, and health time, so a descriptor id that resolves to a different capability than the one being registered is refused rather than silently trusted; an idempotent lifecycle reconciler (lifecycle_state.py) that keeps paused/accepted state coherent (a rebuild or migration forces accepted:false until the operator re-trials it, and a conformant rebuild that changed the capability's own code is detected as stale and its acceptance revoked rather than carried forward silently) and appends to the acceptance audit log on every re-acceptance; and the D-Layer-1 deterministic capability-invariant battery plus test-quality probes (capability_invariants.py), run at next-phase Step 4 and fail-closed before any live trial. Also scrubs internal decision-record IDs out of emitted content and adds .gitignore coverage for the consent/runtime artifacts these mechanisms generate PLUS the Cut 1.1 slice: five sets of improvements to bulk-write reliability, capability-lifecycle testing, and audit trust. First, resuming an interrupted bulk run (a large batch of writes split into many smaller pieces) now picks up safely from exactly where it left off, using the one approval you already gave, recorded at the moment you gave it — it can never restart from the beginning, silently skip pieces, or ask you to approve the same batch twice. Second, a capability's own tests can no longer accidentally depend on leftover state from an unrelated earlier run, and building a new capability now proves — as a standard build-time check — that its tests would actually catch a real mistake, not just that they run without error; approving a capability also now cleans up any leftover internal marker it no longer needs, and a naming collision between a capability you have only described and one you have actually built is reported honestly rather than showing as broken. Third, finishing an approval that had a related pending update task now also completes that task instead of leaving it stranded, and a capability an update safely paused can be brought back through the same guided review-and-approve flow used to build a new one. Fourth, you can now ask your system, at any time, exactly what a bulk run wrote, what is recoverable, and whether it can confirm each write against the real account — without waiting on a scheduled check — and a handful of routine, read-only checks (that never write anything) no longer interrupt you for permission every single time; anything that writes anywhere still always asks first. Fifth, a privacy-safe, committable summary of each bulk run is now saved automatically — with no raw personal information in it — so you always have durable proof of what happened and that you approved it, and a run's outcome is now always described strictly from what actually happened, so it can never be rounded up to “all done” when it was not. PLUS the v0.16.0 Cut 1.2 slice: an emitted-lib-only, minor-additive slice (Cut 1.2 — core-integrity findings A' / V15-3b) touching exactly two files in the emitted external_write lib (agents/lib/external_write/): scan.py adds a STRUCTURAL CAPABILITY-zone import-boundary check — capability/operator code may only reach the bulk-write engine through its small, approved allowlist (capability_api, operations, read_facade); any other internal external_write submodule import is now refused as a sealed_kernel_import bypass, closing the routing-invariant class (V15-3) at the module level instead of one remembered symbol at a time. zones.py extends the SEALED_KERNEL exemption registry with eight pre-existing deterministic, non-vendor, non-credential kernel support modules that the new rule would otherwise have flagged, so legitimate internal kernel wiring is recognized rather than mistaken for a violation. No other emitted content changes.) No breaking changes; all existing capabilities are byte-for-byte unaffected and all prior operator project files remain valid.
migrations.preflight: Verify the operator project carries a .wizard/manifest.json whose foundation_bundle_version is v0.10.2 (or any version in the supported range). A backup is taken automatically.
migrations.rollback: Restore from .wizard/backups/pre-v0.16.0/ and set foundation_bundle_version back to v0.10.2.

First, resuming an interrupted bulk run (a large batch of writes split into many smaller pieces) now picks up safely from exactly where it left off, using the one approval you already gave, recorded at the moment you gave it — it can never restart from the beginning, silently skip pieces, or ask you to approve the same batch twice. Second, a capability's own tests can no longer accidentally depend on leftover state from an unrelated earlier run, and building a new capability now proves — as a standard build-time check — that its tests would actually catch a real mistake, not just that they run without error; approving a capability also now cleans up any leftover internal marker it no longer needs, and a naming collision between a capability you have only described and one you have actually built is reported honestly rather than showing as broken. Third, finishing an approval that had a related pending update task now also completes that task instead of leaving it stranded, and a capability an update safely paused can be brought back through the same guided review-and-approve flow used to build a new one. Fourth, you can now ask your system, at any time, exactly what a bulk run wrote, what is recoverable, and whether it can confirm each write against the real account — without waiting on a scheduled check — and a handful of routine, read-only checks (that never write anything) no longer interrupt you for permission every single time; anything that writes anywhere still always asks first. Fifth, a privacy-safe, committable summary of each bulk run is now saved automatically — with no raw personal information in it — so you always have durable proof of what happened and that you approved it, and a run's outcome is now always described strictly from what actually happened, so it can never be rounded up to “all done” when it was not. PLUS the v0.16.0 Cut 1.2 slice: an emitted-lib-only, minor-additive slice (Cut 1.2 — core-integrity findings A' / V15-3b) touching exactly two files in the emitted external_write lib (agents/lib/external_write/): scan.py adds a STRUCTURAL CAPABILITY-zone import-boundary check — capability/operator code may only reach the bulk-write engine through its small, approved allowlist (capability_api, operations, read_facade); any other internal external_write submodule import is now refused as a sealed_kernel_import bypass, closing the routing-invariant class (V15-3) at the module level instead of one remembered symbol at a time. zones.py extends the SEALED_KERNEL exemption registry with eight pre-existing deterministic, non-vendor, non-credential kernel support modules that the new rule would otherwise have flagged, so legitimate internal kernel wiring is recognized rather than mistaken for a violation. No other emitted content changes.) No breaking changes; all existing capabilities are byte-for-byte unaffected and all prior operator project files remain valid.
migrations.preflight: Verify the operator project carries a .wizard/manifest.json whose foundation_bundle_version is v0.7.0 (or any version in the supported range). A backup is taken automatically.
migrations.rollback: Restore from .wizard/backups/pre-v0.16.0/ and set foundation_bundle_version back to v0.7.0.

migrations.migration_steps: 1. Run the update check (it reports what's new + the recommendation). 2. On the operator's yes, the assistant applies it ("${{WIZARD_HOME:-$HOME/agent-wizard}}/scripts/wizard" self-upgrade --to v0.16.0 --apply). 3. A backup is taken first; the operator's own data, rules, credentials, and logs are never touched.
migrations.notes: Minor-additive combined jump from v0.6.9 straight to v0.15.0: delivers everything through v0.12.0 (the RunEnvelope bulk-write trust core) PLUS the v0.13.0 operability slice (a turnkey operator-acceptance CLI (registered_adapters.py fires every adapter module's registration up front), the judgment-path triage tool (triage.py) bound to a versioned reviewed_set-v2 so an operator's approval is bound to exactly what was shown, a safe standing-automation entrypoint that never lets a scheduled job silently fall through to a live run on an unrecognized flag, and an offline OAuth scope grant-check (deny-by-default: verified only after a real, recorded use of exactly the declared scope) surfaced through the credential registry, Credential Setup, and the next-phase live-trial-readiness gate) PLUS the v0.13.1 patch (the upgrade reconcile now also scopes agents/capabilities/ so a retired-surface capability whose imports are broken is paused as import-broken rather than left merely "paused"; a new composite, read-only capability health-check primitive (capability_health.py) lets a session-start orientation check catch an import-broken capability before inviting the operator into it; the write_gate runtime now fails closed on a paused capability's op_kind on every sanctioned route; and the update-notice prose now separates the informational "see what's new" read from the separate, explicit approval an apply requires) PLUS the v0.14.0 Phase 3 Cut 1 self-QA slice (a fail-closed capability-identity resolver (capability_identity.py) enforcing a four-way descriptor/capability/mechanism/module-stem coherence check — surface deliberately excluded — at registration, acceptance, and health time, so a descriptor id that resolves to a different capability than the one being registered is refused rather than silently trusted; an idempotent lifecycle reconciler (lifecycle_state.py) that keeps paused/accepted state coherent (a rebuild or migration forces accepted:false until the operator re-trials it, and a conformant rebuild that changed the capability's own code is detected as stale and its acceptance revoked rather than carried forward silently) and appends to the acceptance audit log on every re-acceptance; and the D-Layer-1 deterministic capability-invariant battery plus test-quality probes (capability_invariants.py), run at next-phase Step 4 and fail-closed before any live trial. Also scrubs internal decision-record IDs out of emitted content and adds .gitignore coverage for the consent/runtime artifacts these mechanisms generate PLUS the Cut 1.1 slice: five sets of improvements to bulk-write reliability, capability-lifecycle testing, and audit trust. First, resuming an interrupted bulk run (a large batch of writes split into many smaller pieces) now picks up safely from exactly where it left off, using the one approval you already gave, recorded at the moment you gave it — it can never restart from the beginning, silently skip pieces, or ask you to approve the same batch twice. Fourth, you can now ask your system, at any time, exactly what a bulk run wrote, what is recoverable, and whether it can confirm each write against the real account — without waiting on a scheduled check — and a handful of routine, read-only checks (that never write anything) no longer interrupt you for permission every single time; anything that writes anywhere still always asks first. Fifth, a privacy-safe, committable summary of each bulk run is now saved automatically — with no raw personal information in it — so you always have durable proof of what happened and that you approved it, and a run's outcome is now always described strictly from what actually happened, so it can never be rounded up to “all done” when it was not.
